headline: US-Cuba Secret Talks Suggested by Russian Oil Tanker Arrival and Prisoner Release
deck: A sanctioned Russian oil tanker docked in Cuba and unloaded 700,000 barrels of crude, following a shift in President Trump's stance on Cuban oil imports—sparking speculation of quiet diplomatic progress.
body: A sanctioned Russian oil tanker, the Anatoly Kolodkin, docked at Cuba’s Matanzas oil terminal on Tuesday, unloading 700,000 barrels of crude oil. This significant event, coupled with Cuba's release of over 2,000 prisoners, signals potential progress in previously undisclosed negotiations between Washington and Havana.
The arrival of the Russian vessel marks a notable shift from past US policy. In January, US President Donald Trump had emphatically stated on social media:
“THERE WILL BE NO MORE OIL OR MONEY GOING TO CUBA – ZERO!”

Just last week, however, President Trump softened his position, telling reporters, “If a country wants to send some oil into Cuba right now, I have no problem with it.” This statement effectively cleared the path for the Anatoly Kolodkin.
Concurrently, Cuba announced the release of 2,010 prisoners on Thursday. The Cuban government framed this mass release as a humanitarian gesture coinciding with Holy Week.

Observers are linking both the tanker's arrival and the prisoner release, interpreting them as concrete evidence of ongoing, sensitive negotiations between Washington and Havana. These actions suggest reciprocal good-will gestures.

William LeoGrande, a professor of government at the American University in Washington, notes, “It suggests that the two sides may be making reciprocal gestures of good will to advance the conversations they have been having.”

The economic situation in Cuba has grown increasingly dire under the weight of the US oil blockade. Most petrol stations across the island are closed, and daily blackouts have become a persistent reality for its 9.5 million inhabitants.

The vital Cuban tourism sector is described as "all but dead." Major airlines from Canada, Russia, China, and France have ceased operations, with Iberia also scheduled to depart by the end of May, further isolating the island.

This severe economic pressure has contributed to a significant demographic shift, with an estimated 2 million Cubans having left the island in the last five years alone. This exodus underscores the profound challenges facing the nation.

While many see a direct link to negotiations, an unnamed ambassador suggests an alternative: “One option is that it’s a tactical move by the White House... So that as the humanitarian emergency worsens they can point to something specific they did – even though we know it’s nothing in the grand scheme of things.”
Another unnamed diplomat offers a more optimistic view, stating, “Or it could mean there is a bit of progress on negotiations. And this is a confidence-building measure.”

In a related development, another Russian tanker, the Sea Horse, carrying 200,000 barrels of Russian fuel, diverted its course to Venezuela as the Anatoly Kolodkin reached Cuba. This highlights the broader geopolitical dynamics of Russian energy distribution in the region.
